data.text:
INT: Tape 15 for Mark Verstandig. You're talking about Dr. Eisenberg. 

MV: Ah, yeah. Eisenberg. Now, he told me, look, the Germans didn't break the law, he said. I remember when they burned the Jews alive in the synagogues, they used to say in Mielec that there were unruly elements in the German army doing such things. 

Unruly government-- an unruly governments in the German army is a fiction. There are no unruly Germans. They broke the law, but they had orders to do it and they did it in many, many Jewish towns. And they did it because they wanted to tell the Jews, keep your mouth shut. Be quiet. Otherwise you will finish up like them. 

And that was a lesson to the Poles. What we did to the Jews today, we may do it tomorrow. But the Poles still didn't understand this lesson of burning people alive in the synagogue. 

What do you mean, he told me? Breaking the law? The Kristallnacht was breaking the law. They broke the law, but they had orders to do it. And that is how this new Germany is behaving. And with this appeal, you will finish up in a concentration camp, and your sister will finish up in a concentration camp. Tear her to pieces. Don't do it. 

I will make for you an application where I will beg the authorities to help your sister because you are-- you-- you understand that it is good for the German Reich and for the army that it should be in the hand of a [NON-ENGLISH]. But you need something to eat and so on and so on, that they will do it. I will do it for you. 

And he prepared for me such a petition. And he said but between you and me, I wouldn't even send in this petition either. Forget about it, and I did. That was a-- a life-saving lesson that I had from this Dr. Eisenberg. It showed to me how-- how the position of the Jews was. 

They thought that they are living-- that they live a normal life. But that was an illusion. It started with burning Jews alive in the synagogues, and it finished with an expulsion of the Jews on the 9th of December, 1942. They called in the people. 

There-- there was-- I mentioned it before, that-- that-- that Dr. [NON-ENGLISH] went-- there were rumors that something is going to happen, and Dr. [NON-ENGLISH] went to the Landrat, and the Landrat didn't-- they said it's not true. And the deputy Landrat called him in and tendered him over a pack of blank permits to-- to-- blank travel permits and told save-- save your life, and disseminate to the Jews that they should run for their life. 

Something is going to happen. And he showed them that-- with a finger on the calendar-- it's going to happen on Monday. And that was why-- I-- I was living with Dr. [NON-ENGLISH], that I took him to Polaniec and so on. But that was in the-- in December-- in the-- they-- they-- when-- when Dr. [NON-ENGLISH] run away with me. 

And others took over. The Landrat already could see that-- that the people know already that something is going to happen. So they ask them for ransom. If you-- if you-- if you deliver so-- so much and so much in foreign currency, gold, and so on, everything will be fine. Everything was delivered. 

And on Monday the 9th of-- of March, the-- the whole-- the whole-- the whole shtetl was surrounded by police. Jews called out to the main square. And the young people were-- were-- were taken to concentration camps, and to-- and to-- and to labor camps, where the elderly people were marched to the airport, to-- there was-- to the fields of the airport. It was not an airport. There was-- there were factories making airplanes, Hermann Goring worker. 

They called and there were-- they were marched seven kilometers towards [POLISH]. And while they were standing outside in snow in the 19th-- in the 9th of March, one spotted my father with-- he-- who wore high boots. Everybody in Mielec wore high boots in the snow and so on. They were-- were Polish boots. 

He said, look, I like these boots. Take them off. And my father had to march barefoot seven kilometers in snow. And there in [POLISH], people were put into hangers. There was my wife there and my family, my auntie, my cousins, and my sisters, my-- no, there was not my-- my-- and the auntie was-- the aunt-- the aunt and her-- and her daughters were there. And my wife's parents were there. They were-- they were placed in hangers. 

And outside the same day of-- when they arrived, they took out the elderly people and they killed-- they shot about 1,500 people on the spot. Called in the young people from the labor camp a few kilometers further along to dig a-- to to dig a mass grave. And they put them into the mass grave-- into-- into the grave over there. And all other people went-- were-- were left in-- in these hangers, and daily-- were daily selections were still going on. Daily selections through the hangers. You have to come. 

I had some documents where-- under which I-- from which I learned why these daily selections were-- were done. Because they had to-- they had to-- they prepared places to send over-- to travel-- 4,500 people. So they needed to reduce the number in these hangers to 4,500. 

So one day they spotted-- they-- they spotted [POLISH], a judge in Mielec. Was a very interesting personality, this [POLISH]. It's amazing that they didn't spot him while they were marching, because he was an invalid with a wooden leg. 

And he must have got this position as a judge because he was in the-- in Pilsudski's legion. He was a legionnaire. So this is the reason why he-- he was-- he was the only Jewish judge in-- in Mielec. Was a very intelligent man, and a very capable man. A very capable man. 

And was a tragedy over there. They called him out. It's amazing. They called out this judge, and-- to be shot. And his wife and his daughter-- was-- his daughter must have been 12 years old-- were walking behind him begging the SS men to spare his life. But they was outside the door, and somehow this judge broke down. Unbelievable. 

He broke down. He started in a high-pitched voice to beg. He said-- he addressed the German SS's, the German [GERMAN], addressed them. [SPEAKING GERMAN], divine-- something like divine masters. What do you want from me? I lost my leg fighting the Bolsheviks, fighting the communists. I was in the Austrian army. 

And the answer was, you [GERMAN] Jude, [INAUDIBLE] Jew. And they shot him and he fell down. And he fell down. And his wife and daughter asked to be shot, and they obliged. They shot his wife and his daughter. 

Was very-- while-- while-- it-- while-- while writing this-- while writing down this episode in my book, as I mentioned, Primo Levi, who said that there are seldom people who they-- who know how to go to their deaths with dignity. And somehow there are not those people you would suppose-- you-- you would believe they would-- 

On the other hand, there was another episode over there. A certain Jew called Horowitz from a rabbinical family, and he came back from Germany through [NON-ENGLISH]. And throughout the-- the-- 1938, that's a-- a different story. 

So he was there with his daughter. His daughter was about 22, 23 years old, born in Germany. They were thrown out from Germany. And they called this Mr. Horowitz out to be shot in this selection. He went so-- he went very stoically, very-- very quietly went up. And they shot him. 

And behind him was his daughter, and she was very elegantly dressed like it was at that time, the-- the fashion. She wore a white glove on her left hand, and she kept the other glove on-- on-- in the-- in the hand-- in the hand. That was the fashion that the right hand should be without a glove in case you are-- you are shaking hands with somebody. She was walking with him, having-- with these white gloves, and asked the German to do her a favor, to shoot her too. And the German shoot her. 

But somehow he didn't-- he didn't kill her immediately. She put her hand against her breast, which was full of blood, and threw the glove. [SPEAKING GERMAN]. Thank you. There is no-- there is no room for two people like you and me to live on this planet. 

It's a very interesting story because it shows you two different [INAUDIBLE], how they are-- how they went to their deaths. 

INT: What happened to-- 

MV: And you would-- and you would not have suspected. 

INT: What happened to the rest of your family there? 

MV: Beg your pardon? 

INT: What happened to the rest of your family? 

MV: The rest of my family. 

Yeah. They were-- yeah, I remember now. There were the two sisters over there. 

And there was my wife, her parents. Was my auntie. And her daughters. I'm-- I was-- in the beginning I thought, no, no, that my sisters were not-- they came later-- no, they came earlier back to Mielec. They were not in Mielec. They came earlier. 

I sent them-- I received from the same deputy Landrat who tried to save the Jews, but the Jews didn't believe. He'd given them permits, travel permits, that they should run away and save themselves. Now, through Antony Droba, I got the permits from this on-- Anthony went to him, and he gave him permits. Blank. 

He sent the permits to me. I filled them out and send all the permits to my family in Lubyanka. They went to a place called [RUSSIAN] where-- by train. There, horse carts were waiting for them, and they took them to a small town called Lubyanka. A town like Mielec. 

People lived there quite-- quietly. Like nothing would have happened to them. And they came back. 

I sent them this to-- to-- they came back to Polaniec because I was already in Polaniec. Not that Polaniec was a better place, a safer place, than Lubyanka. But they came back to Polaniec because I was there, and it was closer-- put it closer home, closer to Mielec. 

Now, where we lived in Polaniec, one day-- we lived in a house or-- or in a building which belongs to a-- to a family of [INAUDIBLE]. One day, a-- I was sitting close in the post office. And the post office was not far, on a bench. And a fellow came by on a bike. I looked at him. He looked at me. He didn't say a word. I didn't say a word. I knew who he-- who he was, and he knew who I am. 

That was Vladislav Jasinszki, the famous [NON-ENGLISH], a commander of AK for the whole district, Mielec, Polaniec, Tarnow, and so on. There was a-- an-- an award for his head, dead or alive on-- everywhere in the-- in the area. 

I recognized him. He recognized me. He didn't say-- I knew that he was an antisemite. But somehow we met before in Mielec. While I was in Mielec we met once. And he was quite friendly. But here, we passed. He went to the post office. And I was thinking, what can I do? Should I stay here sitting, or should I disappear? 

If I disappear, he may think that I went to the authorities. And then I laughed. I said, what authorities? There are no authorities in Polaniec. There is nobody in Polaniec. 

No-- no authority in-- with-- within 30, 40 kilometers. So I was sitting. He went out. He came back straight to me. We shook hands. I said, look, I must tell you you're in trouble. 

If you think that Mielec was just an exception, you are mistaken. They are killing Jews on the [GERMAN]. Means on the other side of the Polish-- where-- where the Polish border. They are killing them on the spot. I may be able to help you. 

And he told me that he's going to arrange meeting me. But-- where I live. I told him where I live. He said, look, you will be [INAUDIBLE] We will meet in a valley, on the embankment of the Vistula. 

And so it happened that one day I was sitting over there. Somebody came in. They said, look, could you-- could you offer me a light on a cigarette? He wants a cigarette. Yes. You are Mark? I said yes. Today or tomorrow at 2 o'clock on the embankment of the Vistula. 

And then I came there. He told me, look, bring me photos of your family, of you and your family. What there must be people in your family, your wife who speaks Polish all right. Only for-- for people who speak perfect Polish. And I will make for you-- I'll make for you the Aryan papers. 

I brought him next time my picture and picture of my wife. He looks-- and he said, look, I know this Frieda, Frieda [INAUDIBLE]. I know her. That's her. She will not be able to-- to survive in the open. That's it. She looks too Jewish. Her eyes are black. Brown, dark hair. She looks 100% like-- like-- like her forefathers in-- in-- like her forefathers 2,000 years in-- 2,000 years ago in Palestine. 

As far as you are concerned, he told me, I'll give you papers. But remember only in big cities. Never-- never live in a small city. Only in big cities. In a small city, you'll-- they will immediately know a-- that a stranger came, and they will start looking who he is and so on. 

And for your wife, he told me, she will have to-- how he called it? To-- to-- to dye her hair. And the [INAUDIBLE] was a-- a-- peroxide. She will have to peroxide her hair. She will be a Titian beauty. 

And so I-- and I will prepare a place for her in the forest, a safe-- a safe house, 100% safe. And at the same time, while I was waiting sometimes for him to come-- he never came on time. Never on time. He-- always sometimes wait for him an hour, sometimes two hours. 

I met there two very interesting fellows. Two elderly gentlemen, Poles. I don't remember their names. And they told me who they are. That they are in charge of making [INAUDIBLE] for artillery shells for the army, out of [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], yeah? Which were-- which are grown here along the Vistula. 

They live in Warsaw. And before the war, they were high officials in the Ministry of Agriculture. They are both engineers. One is a chemist and the other is another engineer. I don't remember. 

And we were talking-- we were meeting very often on this embankment. And one day they told me, look, I'll tell you something. I have to-- to repay a debt to Jews. In 1905, after the revolution, we had to run for our life. And we left the Congress Poland to Silesia, which was under the German-- under the German occupation. And there a Jewish family kept us for two years without a penny. Not a socialist, just a Jewish family from Bunde. 

And we live-- we lived there, and they-- they supplied us with lodge and board for two years without a penny. Sooner or later you will have to run away from Polaniec. This is our address. Come to us. You will live with us. You'll look good. You live with us. I don't want anything. I want just to repay my debt. 

And so it happened that the time came for Polaniec. They prepared-- Vladislav Jasinszki prepared a safe house for my wife in the forest-- in the forest, by a family called [POLISH], I think. They-- they were forest rangers. Forest rangers. 

And their daughter-- I knew their daughter. Their daughter were-- worked in the post office. And she was a member of the AK. And that was how he arranged this-- this with the AK. And she went over there. And I went to Warsaw. 

And I also remember how I went to Warsaw. I had a suitcase with all my-- with-- with all my belongings. And I traveled first of all with-- with-- with this long [NON-ENGLISH]. Narrow lane. Narrow-- narrow railway lines. Train-- train-- yeah, to another station in [POLISH] or something like that. And there I boarded the train to Warsaw. 

And I remember I opened the door and there was-- was-- plenty-- plenty of people were there. And I pushed myself with all the chutzpah in the world. I said only with-- with chutzpah you will play the-- you'll-- you-- you will be right. I pushed myself into a seat, and my suitcase close to me, and later I pushed the top because-- before-- after I got my seat. And there I was sitting among a lot of people, all Polish people were-- Polish people, [NON-ENGLISH], were [NON-ENGLISH]. 

There-- they were-- they were carrying sausages and eggs and butter to Warsaw and to the black market and so on. And Polish people around. I said-- next to the window was sitting looks to me like kind of an intellectual, was reading a book. And glasses on, a young-- a young man. 

And the talk was about Jews. Jews and Jews and Jews, and Germans are killing them. We'll be soon-- soon we'll get rid of them. They-- and one-- one-- soon we'll get rid of them. And the fellow sitting next to the window said to them, look, if there-- it has been arranged already that the Jews will survive, will be settled in Russia next-- close to Odessa. There will-- all Polish Jews will be there. 

And that's true. Was such an arrangement. Means-- means they got the minister-- [INAUDIBLE] and a-- and a-- and a-- and a representative of the Jewish, Dr. [NON-ENGLISH] with Professor [NON-ENGLISH]. I didn't know about it. The first time I heard about it was from this fellow. The Jews will go to Odessa, to the [INAUDIBLE]. 

And one woman jumped out. Not Odessa! No, no, no. Only there they-- if they go to Odessa, they will come back like rats, and they will suck our blood. No Odessa. Palestine! 

And I said it is high time that I should start talking because otherwise I will be-- I-- I-- I will be suspect. And I told them, look? What's going on with us, with us Poles? The schools are closed. The university professors are in concentration camps. Even schoolteachers are in concentration camps. 

Our people growing up without schools. And the only thing you have got in mind are Jews, Jews, Jews. You are playing the part of the Nazis. And that will harm our countries when the peace talks will-- when they start peace talks after the war. 

There was silence all over. And one woman said, you see? A Jewish uncle. That is a Jew uncle. This-- this-- this is a [POLISH], something like-- I said-- I said I passed my exam. I'm not a Jew. I'm only Jew uncle. That is how I arrived. But we never-- we didn't talk any more-- any more about the Jews until we came to Warsaw. That is how I went to Warsaw. 

INT: Could I just ask you, do you think that sort of mentality was common amongst Polish people? 

MV: Oh, yes. There was not a-- there was not a single meeting of Poles without talking about Jews. And I will tell you further when I came to Warsaw, I went-- I went to this-- I took a-- a cab. I went to-- to this house not far from the [POLISH] street. I don't remember where it was, nor the number, but it's not far from [POLISH] street. 

And the concierge, the [POLISH], the gatekeeper was sitting at the gate. And he asked me where to? I told him, it's-- I'm going to my uncle. I know my way. Because I knew where to go. Right, left, and so on. I-- I-- I was here before the war. I am going to my uncle. Now-- 

INT: Thank you. We'll continue next tape.
